title: Aygen Triumphs in Montreal as Sam Fact Stuns the Paddock and Keisner Seizes the Championship Lead
meta: Published: 18 May 2026 | By Elena Vasquez, Chief Race Correspondent | F1CC News
caption: Eren Aygen celebrates his third career victory — all from P6 on the grid — as Williams reclaim the constructors' lead.
body:
The rain came softly at first, a light mist that shimmered in the Montreal sunshine, then faded as quickly as it had arrived, leaving behind a drying track, a reshuffled order, and a race that no one could have predicted. Eren Aygen won the Canadian Grand Prix. He started sixth. He has never won from any position higher than sixth. The stat was mentioned to him after the race by the television crew, and he laughed, shaking his head in disbelief. "I didn't know that," he said, and the paddock laughed with him. But the victory was no joke. It was a statement — Williams' third win of the season, a result that vaults them back to the top of the constructors' championship, and a performance that marks Aygen as one of the most unpredictable, irresistible forces in the sport.

Behind him, Robin Keisner drove from 13th to second, a recovery that gives him the championship lead — though the asterisk of Audi's Miami disqualification still hangs over the standings, and the tables shown to the television audience after the race flickered between conflicting versions of the truth. Sam Fact, the man who had not scored a single point all season, took a stunning third place for Aston Martin, his first podium since Bahrain 2025, his first points of the year. The hard-tyre gamble had paid off. The garage erupted. The mechanics wept. And Sam Fact, the driver who had been invisible for seven races, was suddenly the story of the day.

The race had begun under strange conditions — cool air, a damp track, and a grid that defied expectation. Julian Kondra, the Alpine rookie, started from pole position, his first in Formula 1, and held the lead into Turn 1. But the pressure was immense, and the rain was coming. When it arrived, on lap 11, the field scrambled for the pits. Kondra stayed out a lap too long, his Alpine trapped behind traffic, and emerged in the lower reaches of the field. His race ended later in the wall at Turn 5, a heartbreaking end to a weekend that had promised so much. Alpine, who had locked out two of the top three grid positions, left Montreal with nothing — Maradóner nursing mechanical issues to eighth, Kondra walking back to the paddock with his helmet in his hands.

The strategic gamble of the day belonged to those who pitted early for intermediate tyres. Richard Hicks, the Audi rookie, was the first to dive into the pits, and the decision vaulted him into the lead. He held it through the wet phase, defending against Aygen with the composure of a veteran. But when the track dried and the second round of pit stops came, Hicks' race unravelled in the pit lane. A sticking wheel cost him ten seconds, dropping him to tenth at the flag. His teammate, Keisner, who had pitted later and on a different strategy, surged through the field on hard tyres, hunting down Sam Fact and Lukas Wright and Vandesteene with a relentless rhythm. His move on Sam Fact on lap 67 — around the outside of a corner where no one overtakes — was the pass of the race. Second place was the reward. The championship lead was the prize.

Eetu Väisänen finished fifth, a result that felt like a defeat. He had started fourth, stayed out too long during the rain, and lost positions in the pit-stop shuffle. His late charge on fresh tyres closed the gap to the leaders, but the podium was out of reach. "The first pit stop did not go that well with us trying to bank on a bit of a different strategy — wasn't the greatest," he said afterwards. "Definitely the gap hurts being less." The championship lead he has held since Australia has now slipped to Keisner — though the television graphics could not agree on the exact margin, the feeling in the paddock was clear: the momentum has shifted.

Maikel Zwart's misery continued. Starting from the pit lane, he reported mechanical issues mid-race, his voice strained over the radio. "Guys, problems again, it's getting worse now," he said. His engineer replied with the hollow reassurance of a team that has run out of answers. He finished 16th. The Mercedes that had promised so much in pre-season is now a shadow of itself, and Zwart's screams from Jeddah still echo in the memory.

Lukas Wright and Quarter Kevu finished sixth and seventh for Racing Bulls, a solid double points haul but a quiet afternoon by their standards. The team that dominated 2024 and 2025 is still searching for the spark that defined those campaigns. Wright briefly challenged for the podium on soft tyres, but his rubber faded, and Sam Fact swept past. Kevu, as always, was consistent, but consistency alone does not win championships when others are winning races.

After the race, Eren Aygen stood by the television booth, still in his race suit, and spoke with the calm of a driver who has been here before. "It feels incredible," he said. "Actually every time I won, I didn't start from the first row or the second row — maybe it's the thing. When I take pole, it's like the worst race of the time. I don't know — maybe I'm just going to skip qualifying and win every race." He laughed. The stat about P6 was presented to him. He didn't know it. Now he does. It will follow him to Monaco, where he will try to break the curse — or lean into it.

The paddock packs up in the Montreal dusk, the St. Lawrence River glittering beyond the circuit. The championship has a new leader. Williams are constructors' leaders again. Sam Fact has a podium. Alpine have a broken heart. And the circus heads to Monaco, where the walls are closer than ever, and the season's next chapter waits to be written. Eren Aygen won from P6. Again. Some patterns, it seems, are not meant to be broken.
